reason: A row in a relational table is a record (tuple); field/column are vertical, 'entry' is a cell.
DROP TABLE removes the table object; DELETE only removes rows and leaves the table.
In the relational model a relation is realized as a table (rows=tuples, columns=attributes).
A Network Operating System provides file/printer/resource sharing services; a router forwards packets, IOS is a vendor OS.
Different IP networks require layer-3 forwarding; switch/hub/bridge are layer-1/2 and cannot route between subnets.
MAC addresses are globally unique via IEEE-assigned OUI plus vendor-assigned suffix.
Ripple carry is O(n) gate delay; carry look-ahead computes carries in parallel, O(log n)/constant-depth, so it is faster.
Neither TCP nor UDP provides security; the true answer is a VPN/tunnel. Of the two extracted options TCP is the least-wrong (connection-oriented, carries TLS). Option set appears truncated.
Scalability is the capacity to handle growing data/load; extensibility is about adding features/schema capability.
Single option, no distractors. Text matches data-flow (def-use) testing: define a variable then use/modify it. Plausible but unverifiable as a discriminating question.
Single option. 'Datagram' is ambiguous: UDP datagram = transport layer, but Kurose/Forouzan call the network-layer PDU a datagram. Transport is defensible via UDP but the item is not clean.
1 TB = 10^12 bytes = one trillion bytes; flash/storage are media, not a unit.
32 bits / 8 bits per byte = 4 bytes per memory transfer.
Full mesh gives every node multiple independent paths; ring has two, star has one via the hub.
Single option but factually the BST ordering invariant: left subtree < node < right subtree.
510 = 2^9 - 2, so 9 host bits -> /23 -> 255.255.254.0. Arithmetic checks out.
Layer 1 (Physical) transmits raw bit streams over the medium.
A Java exception is an event thrown at run time that disrupts normal flow; compile-time problems are compilation errors, not exceptions.

Standard IT-literacy answer: buy software that meets your system/hardware requirements. But option B (compatibility with software you already use) is nearly as defensible and no key exists, so this is contestable.
unanswerable - UDP is a transport-layer protocol and has no physical coverage distance; stem is garbled (likely mis-transcribed from a wireless/media question) and there are no options.
